White balance and Virtual ColorChecker transformation are defined.

value.color_calibration: Compute camera RGB and CIE XYZ values for 24 ColorChecker patches and derive a transformation matrix
value.illuminant_normalization: Normalize the illuminant spectrum using camera-channel maximum sensitivity
value.white_balance: Normalize sensor intensity by direct-light channel response

evidence.support: The implementation requires known camera sensitivity and specifies the camera model.
The Virtual ColorChecker and camera-to-XYZ transformation are described.
The paper explicitly states that camera information is required prior knowledge.

value: Camera spectral sensitivity is known before inference.
The model uses Canon 5D Mark II RGB sensitivity over 400–720 nm for the reported implementation.
A color transformation can be calculated from camera RGB responses and ColorChecker XYZ values.

evidence.support: The acquisition system and reported optical priors are specified.

capture_protocol.review_notes: The paper does not provide a device-agnostic mobile capture protocol.

melanin.parameterization: Volume fraction, extinction coefficient, and concentration

evidence.support: The source defines epidermal melanin absorption, dermal hemoglobin absorption, and reduced scattering.
The image-formation equations combine illuminant, reflectance, camera sensitivity, diffuse light, and specular light.

layers.dermis: Hemoglobin absorption layer with multiple scattering

value.relationship: Skin reflectance is generated from epidermal transmission and dermal reflection, then combined with illuminant SPD and camera spectral sensitivity to produce RGB values.

evidence.support: The abstract identifies the limitations of clinical equipment and states the proposed decomposition and reconstruction approach.
The introduction describes dedicated acquisition systems and motivates a more extensible model.

problem_addressed.value: Existing clinical skin-analysis systems can estimate melanin and hemoglobin reliably but are acquisition-system-specific, costly, and computationally intensive. The paper addresses image-based recovery of skin-property maps while preserving the input image resolution.

evidence.support: The discussion explicitly identifies comparison, detail, morphology, prior-knowledge, dataset, and SPD limitations.

value: The model requires prior knowledge of the light-source SPD and camera information.
VISIA and the proposed method produce maps in different output formats, requiring conversion before comparison.
Postprocessing and normalization choices can influence correlation.
The proposed maps capture less fine detail than VISIA.
The specular map does not yet preserve all morphological detail needed for wrinkle or pore diagnosis.
Broader datasets and more accurate spectrometer-based SPD measurements are needed to improve accuracy.

evidence.support: The source reports reconstruction PSNR and map behavior over training.
The source reports positive correlations with VISIA and identifies VISIA's superior fine-detail capture.

reported_results.review_notes: Exact per-map correlation values are not asserted here because the selected packet output does not expose them unambiguously as plain text.

value.clinical_comparison: Predicted melanin and hemoglobin maps show strong positive relationships with VISIA maps.
value.map_detail: VISIA captures finer detailed features than the proposed model.
value.reconstruction: Average PSNR exceeds 30, which the authors interpret as high similarity between input and reconstructed images.
value.training_interpretation: Because VISIA maps were not used during training, the authors interpret the correlation as evidence that the network learned aspects of the intended light–tissue interaction.

evidence.support: The subject counts and train-test division are stated.

value.population_demographics: Not sufficiently established in the selected source ranges
value.unit_of_analysis: Subject facial images and spatial skin maps

evidence.support: The skin-layer model identifies scattering structures and defines reduced-scattering parameters.
The objective and four-map decomposition are stated in the abstract.
The stated contribution is a model trained using the forward light–tissue interaction.

scientific_objective.value: Train a deep network to solve a skin light–tissue forward problem by decomposing facial RGB images into melanin, hemoglobin, shading, and specular maps and reconstructing the input image from those components.

evidence.support: The source explicitly defines four spatial output maps.

value.spatial_form: Pixel-aligned two-dimensional maps at the input image resolution

evidence.support: The physical, chromophore, scattering, camera, illuminant, and image-formation mechanisms are explicit.
The paper explicitly retains camera and illuminant information as required priors.

value.boundary: The method operates with known illuminant SPD and known camera spectral sensitivity. It does not establish blind recovery under unknown lighting and unknown device response, nor a device-independent mobile observability certificate.
directly_relevant_mechanisms: Physics-constrained decomposition of RGB skin images
Illuminant-SPD and camera-sensitivity conditioned image formation

evidence.support: The held-out subjects, clinical system, reference-map preparation, and metrics are defined.
The source states that VISIA results were not used in training and interprets the positive comparison.

comparisons: Predicted melanin map versus VISIA melanin map
Predicted hemoglobin map versus VISIA hemoglobin map
value.training_independence: VISIA melanin and hemoglobin results were not used as training ground truth

title: Deep learning-based optical approach for skin analysis of melanin and hemoglobin distribution.
